title: SemRec: Cross-Language Equivalence Verification for C↔Rust Migration
consensus_strengths: σ-bridge coercion layer correctly captures source-level semantic divergences (C UB vs Rust wrapping) that LLVM IR-level tools erase — 13/16 IR-invisible divergences demonstrate the necessity.
CEGAR LLM-in-the-loop repair achieving 45% verification success with 17 bugs found is a pragmatically useful contribution.
Ablation showing σ-bridge halves the error rate (80% → 40%) provides clear evidence of its necessity.
Product program construction with language-specific semantic encoding is a clean formalization of the cross-language equivalence problem.
consensus_weaknesses: Very narrow scope: single functions ~50 lines, integer arithmetic only, no structs/pointers/heap/concurrency — excludes the vast majority of real migration targets.
Hand-written recursive descent parsers for C and Rust are a significant soundness risk — parser bugs could produce unsound results.
84.2% accuracy means 16% incorrect verdicts, and differential testing matches accuracy in ablation (80% vs 80%), weakening the formal verification value proposition.
49K lines of Python across 135 files suggests significant implementation complexity not subjected to tool verification.
Limited mathematical depth — the σ-bridge is primarily a software engineering contribution.
actionable_recommendations: Replace hand-written parsers with established parser frameworks (tree-sitter, libclang, syn for Rust) to reduce parser-related soundness risks.
Expand the benchmark to >100 function pairs from real migration projects to better assess practical utility.
Analyze why 55% of CEGAR functions remain unverified — is it LLM limitation, Z3 timeout, or fundamental semantic gaps?
Compare against differential testing with larger test budgets to establish the marginal value of formal verification over testing.
